subject: Physics
q:
A square loop of side 2.0 cm is placed inside a long solenoid that has 50 turns per centimetre and carries a sinusoidally varying current of amplitude 2.5 A and angular frequency 700 rad s-1. The central axes of the loop and solenoid coincide. The amplitude of the emf induced in the loop is x×10-4 V. The value of x is ___________

( Take, π=227
question:
A square loop of side 2.0 cm is placed inside a long solenoid that has 50 turns per centimetre and carries a sinusoidally varying current of amplitude 2.5 A and angular frequency 700 rad s-1. The central axes of the loop and solenoid coincide. The amplitude of the emf induced in the loop is x×10-4 V. The value of x is ___________

( Take, π=227
solution:
It is given that the current is varying sinusoidally. The current can be written as I=I0sinωt.

The magnetic field through the solenoid B=μ0nI

The flux through the square is ϕ=μ0nIA

The emf is

 ε=μ0nA×d(I0sinωt)dt⇒ε=μ0nAI0ωcosωt

The amplitude of the emf is,

ε=μ0nAI0ω=4π×10-7×5010-2×4×10-4×2.5×700⇒ε=44×10-4 V

subject: Physics
q: In the given figure, an inductor and resistor are connected in series with a battery of emf E volt. Ea2b J s-1 represents the maximum rate at which the energy is stored in the magnetic field (inductor). The numerical value of ba will be _____.
question: In the given figure, an inductor and resistor are connected in series with a battery of emf E volt. Ea2b J s-1 represents the maximum rate at which the energy is stored in the magnetic field (inductor). The numerical value of ba will be _____.
solution:
The formula to calculate the current flowing through an L-R circuit is given by

i=ER1-e-RtL   ...1

The energy stored within the inductor can be calculated as follows:

Einduction =12Li2=12LE2R21-e-RtL2   ...2

The rate of change of energy stored within the inductor is given by

ddtEinduction =21-e-RtL·e-RtL·RL·E2L2R2=E2Re-RtL-e-2RtL   ...3

For maximum rate,

d2Edt2=0   ...4

From equation (3) and (4), it can be written that

ddtE2Re-RtL-e-2RtL =0⇒e-RtL=2e-2RtL  ⇒eRtL=2   ...5

From equation (3) and (5), it can be written, under maximum energy stored condition for the circuit, that

ddtEinduction =E2R2-1-2-2=E24R

Substitute the values of the resistance and compare with the given expression to obtain the values of the parameters.

a=2b=2R=50

Hence, the required ratio is given by

ba=25

subject: Physics
q: There are two long co-axial solenoids of same length $l$. The inner and outer coils have radii $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ and number of turns per unit length $\mathrm{n}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{n}_{2}$, respectively. The ratio of mutual inductance to the self-inductance of the inner-coil is :
question: There are two long co-axial solenoids of same length $l$. The inner and outer coils have radii $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ and number of turns per unit length $\mathrm{n}_{1}$ and $\mathrm{n}_{2}$, respectively. The ratio of mutual inductance to the self-inductance of the inner-coil is :
solution:
The rate of mutual inductance is given by

$$

\mathrm{M}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{n}, \mathrm{n}_{2} \pi \mathrm{r}_{1}^{2} \quad \ldots(\mathrm{i})

$$

The rate of self inductance is given by

$\mathrm{L}=\mu_{0} \mathrm{n}_{1}^{2} \pi \mathrm{r}_{1}^{2} \quad \ldots(\mathrm{ii})$

Dividing (i) by (ii) $\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{L}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{2}}{\mathrm{n}_{1}}$

subject: Physics
q: A coil is suspended in a uniform magnetic field, with the plane of the coil parallel to the magnetic lines of force. When a current is passed through the coil it starts oscillating; it is very difficult to stop. But if an aluminium plate is placed near to the coil, it stops. This is due to :
question: A coil is suspended in a uniform magnetic field, with the plane of the coil parallel to the magnetic lines of force. When a current is passed through the coil it starts oscillating; it is very difficult to stop. But if an aluminium plate is placed near to the coil, it stops. This is due to :
options: development of air current when the plate is placed.
induction of electrical charge on the plate
shielding of magnetic lines of force as aluminium is a paramagnetic material.
electromagnetic induction in the aluminium plate giving rise to electromagnetic damping.
solution: Oscillating coil produces time variable magnetic field. It cause eddy current in the aluminium plate which causes anti-torque on the coil, due to which is stops.

subject: Physics
q: A metal conductor of length $1 \mathrm{~m}$ rotates vertically about one of its ends at angular velocity 5 radians per second. If the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field is $0.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~T}$, then the e.m.f. developed between the two ends of the conductor is
question: A metal conductor of length $1 \mathrm{~m}$ rotates vertically about one of its ends at angular velocity 5 radians per second. If the horizontal component of earth's magnetic field is $0.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~T}$, then the e.m.f. developed between the two ends of the conductor is
options: depends on the nature of the metal used
depends on the intensity of the radiation
depends both on the intensity of the radiation and the metal used
is the same for all metals and independent of the intensity of the radiation.
solution:
emf. developed is given by
$$
\varepsilon_{\text {ind }}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~B} \omega \mathrm{R}^2=50 \mu \mathrm{V} \text {. }
$$

subject: Physics
q: When the current in a coil changes from <math> <mrow> <mtext>5 A</mtext></mrow> </math> to <math> <mrow> <mtext>2 A</mtext></mrow> </math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>0</mtext><mtext>.1 s</mtext></math>, an average voltage of <math> <mrow> <mtext>50</mtext><mtext> </mtext><mtext>V</mtext></mrow> </math> is produced. The self-inductance of the coil is
question: When the current in a coil changes from <math> <mrow> <mtext>5 A</mtext></mrow> </math> to <math> <mrow> <mtext>2 A</mtext></mrow> </math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>0</mtext><mtext>.1 s</mtext></math>, an average voltage of <math> <mrow> <mtext>50</mtext><mtext> </mtext><mtext>V</mtext></mrow> </math> is produced. The self-inductance of the coil is
solution:
Magnetic flux

ϕ=LI ⇒ ∆ϕ∆t=L ∆I∆t

∴ εindaverage= ∆ϕ∆t=L ∆I∆t

⇒ 50=L × 5-20.1

⇒  53=L

⇒   L=1.67 H

subject: Physics
q:
A square shaped coil of area 70 cm2 having 600 turns rotates in a magnetic field of 0.4 Wb m–2, about an axis which is parallel to one of the side of the coil and perpendicular to the direction of field. If the coil completes 500 revolution in a minute, the instantaneous emf when the plane of the coil is inclined at 60° with the field, will be ______ V.

(Take π=227)
question:
A square shaped coil of area 70 cm2 having 600 turns rotates in a magnetic field of 0.4 Wb m–2, about an axis which is parallel to one of the side of the coil and perpendicular to the direction of field. If the coil completes 500 revolution in a minute, the instantaneous emf when the plane of the coil is inclined at 60° with the field, will be ______ V.

(Take π=227)
solution:
Given here, N=600, A=70×10-4 m2, and B=0.4 T

Angular speed of coil is ω=2πn60=500×2π60=100π6 rad s-1

Instantaneous emf in coil is given by ε=-Ndϕdt, where, magnetic flux ϕ=BAcosωt and ωt=30° is angle between A and B.

So, ε=-NdBAcosωtdt=-N-BAωsinωt=NBAωsinωt

Putting the values, we get

ε=600×70×10-4×0.4×100π6×12

=44 V

q: A conducting circular loop made of a thin wire has area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3.5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> It is placed perpendicular to a time-dependent magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0.4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>sin</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>50</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> The field is uniform in space. Then the net charge flowing through the loop during <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>ms</mi></math> is close to
question: A conducting circular loop made of a thin wire has area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3.5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> It is placed perpendicular to a time-dependent magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0.4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>sin</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>50</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> The field is uniform in space. Then the net charge flowing through the loop during <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi>ms</mi></math> is close to
solution:
It is given that, the magnetic field,

B=0.4 sin50πt

and the area of the given circular conducting loop,

A=3.5×10-2 m2

Magnetic flux,

ϕ=BA   ...(1)

Then, induced emf,

ε=dϕdt  ...(2)

When resistance in the circuit is R, the differential charge accumulated, based on current i,

dQ=idt=εRdt=1Rdϕ⇒∆Q=1R ∫dφ=1R ∆ϕ

Substituting given values and using equation (1) and (2),
∆Q=1100.4sin50π×10×10-3-0×3.5×10-2⇒∆Q=0.4×3.5×10-210=1.4×10-3 C

⇒∆Q=1.4 mC

The induced emf at $t=3 \mathrm{~s}$ is
question:
The flux linked with a coil at any instant ' $t$ ' is given by
$\phi=10 t^2-50 t+250$
The induced emf at $t=3 \mathrm{~s}$ is
solution: $e=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-(20 t-50)=-10$ volt

subject: Physics
q:
In the given circuit below inductance values of $L_1$, $L_2$ and $L_3$ are same. The magnetic energy stored in the entire circuit is $(U_t)$ and that stored in the $L_2$ inductor is $(U_l)$. $U_t / U_l$ is __________.
(Ignore the mutual inductance if any)
question:
In the given circuit below inductance values of $L_1$, $L_2$ and $L_3$ are same. The magnetic energy stored in the entire circuit is $(U_t)$ and that stored in the $L_2$ inductor is $(U_l)$. $U_t / U_l$ is __________.
(Ignore the mutual inductance if any)
solution:
Let the inductance of each inductor be $L$, so $L_1 = L_2 = L_3 = L$.

Let the total current flowing through the circuit be $I$. This current flows entirely through $L_1$.

Since $L_2$ and $L_3$ are connected in parallel and have equal inductances, the current $I$ divides equally between them. Therefore, the current through $L_2$ is $\dfrac{I}{2}$ and the current through $L_3$ is $\dfrac{I}{2}$.

The magnetic energy stored in an inductor is given by $U = \dfrac{1}{2} L i^2$.

The energy stored in the inductor $L_2$ is:
$U_l = \dfrac{1}{2} L \left(\dfrac{I}{2}\right)^2 = \dfrac{1}{8} L I^2$

The equivalent inductance of the entire circuit is:
$L_{eq} = L_1 + \dfrac{L_2 L_3}{L_2 + L_3} = L + \dfrac{L \cdot L}{L + L} = L + \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{3L}{2}$

The total magnetic energy stored in the entire circuit is:
$U_t = \dfrac{1}{2} L_{eq} I^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{3L}{2}\right) I^2 = \dfrac{3}{4} L I^2$

The ratio of the total energy to the energy stored in $L_2$ is:
$\dfrac{U_t}{U_l} = \dfrac{\dfrac{3}{4} L I^2}{\dfrac{1}{8} L I^2} = \dfrac{3}{4} \times 8 = 6$

Answer: $6$

subject: Physics
q: A small square loop of wire of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> is placed inside a large square loop of wire of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>. The loops are coplanar and their centers coincide. The value of the mutual inductance of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
question: A small square loop of wire of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>l</mi></math> is placed inside a large square loop of wire of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>L</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>. The loops are coplanar and their centers coincide. The value of the mutual inductance of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>7</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
solution:
Flux linkage for inner loop

ϕ=Bcenter×l2

=4×μ0i4πL2sin45°+sin45°l2

⇒ϕ=22μ0iπLl2

Therefore, mutual inductance will be

M=ϕi=22μ0l2πL=22μ0π

=224ππ×10-7

=82×10-7

=128×10-7 H

Hence, x=128.

q: A square loop of side 20 cm and resistance 1 Ω is moved towards right with a constant speed v0. The right arm of the loop is in a uniform magnetic field of 5 T. The field is perpendicular to the plane of the loop and is going into it. The loop is connected to a network of resistors each of value 4Ω. What should be the value of v0 so that a steady current of 2 mA flows in the loop ?
question: A square loop of side 20 cm and resistance 1 Ω is moved towards right with a constant speed v0. The right arm of the loop is in a uniform magnetic field of 5 T. The field is perpendicular to the plane of the loop and is going into it. The loop is connected to a network of resistors each of value 4Ω. What should be the value of v0 so that a steady current of 2 mA flows in the loop ?
solution:
The EMF is induced in the coil when it is moving in a uniform magnetic field,
e=Bvl and the current, i=eR⇒BlvR=0.002 A
 ⇒v=0.002×RBl=0.002×4+15×0.2=0.01 m s-1=1 cm s-1.

=16×4-4×21000-5005×10-4×10-4
=56×5005×10-8=56×10-6V

B=4π×10-3 T1-t100

The energy dissipated by the coil before the magnetic field is switched off completely is E=______mJ.
question:
A circular conducting coil of radius 1 m is being heated by the change of magnetic field B→ passing perpendicular to the plane in which the coil is laid. The resistance of the coil is 2 μΩ. The magnetic field is slowly switched off such that its magnitude changes in time as  

B=4π×10-3 T1-t100

The energy dissipated by the coil before the magnetic field is switched off completely is E=______mJ.
solution:
ϕ=B→·S→

ϕ=4π×10-31-t100·πR2

ϕ=4×10-3×121-t100

ε=-dϕdt

ε=-ddt4×10-31-t100

ε=4×10-31100=4×10-5 V

When B=0

1-t100=0

t=100 sec

Heat =ε2Rt

Heat =4×10-522×10-6×100 J

Heat =16×10-10×1002×10-6 J

Heat =0.08 J

Heat =80 mJ

subject: Physics
q: At the centre of a fixed large circular coil of radius $\mathrm{R}$, a much smaller circular coil of radius $r$ is placed. The two coils are concentric and are in the same plane. The larger coil carries a current I. The smaller coil is set to rotate with a constant angular velocity $\omega$ about an axis along their common diameter. Calculate the emf induced in the smaller coil after a time $t$ of its start of rotation.
question: At the centre of a fixed large circular coil of radius $\mathrm{R}$, a much smaller circular coil of radius $r$ is placed. The two coils are concentric and are in the same plane. The larger coil carries a current I. The smaller coil is set to rotate with a constant angular velocity $\omega$ about an axis along their common diameter. Calculate the emf induced in the smaller coil after a time $t$ of its start of rotation.
solution:
According to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction,
$$
\begin{array}{r}
e=-\frac{d \phi}{d t} \text { and } \phi=B A \cos \omega t=B \pi r^2 \cos \omega t \\
\Rightarrow \quad e=-\frac{d}{d t}\left(\pi r^2 B \cos \omega t\right)=\pi r^2 B \sin \omega t(\omega) \\
\therefore e=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R} \pi \omega r^2 \sin \omega t\left(\because B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R}\right)
\end{array}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A uniform magnetic field B exists in a direction perpendicular to the plane of a square loop made of a metal wire. The wire has a diameter of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and a total length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The magnetic field changes with time at a steady rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>dB</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>dt</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>032</mn><msup><mi>Ts</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The induced current in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>loop</mo></math>is close to (Resistivity of the metal wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>23</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Ωm</mi></math> )
question: A uniform magnetic field B exists in a direction perpendicular to the plane of a square loop made of a metal wire. The wire has a diameter of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and a total length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The magnetic field changes with time at a steady rate <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>dB</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>dt</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>032</mn><msup><mi>Ts</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The induced current in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>loop</mo></math>is close to (Resistivity of the metal wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>23</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Ωm</mi></math> )
solution:
Radius =d/2=2 mm

Rwire=ρlA

ϕ=BA ; e=dϕdt=dBdtA

i=eR=dBdtA2ρl=0.032×π×2×10-321.23×10-8×4×0.3

=0.61 A

subject: Physics
q: A conducting bar moves on two conducting rails as shown in the figure. A constant magnetic field B exists into the page. The bar starts to move from the vertex at time $t=0$ with a constant velocity. If the induced EMF is $\mathrm{E} \propto \mathrm{t}^{\mathrm{n}}$, then value of n is _.
question: A conducting bar moves on two conducting rails as shown in the figure. A constant magnetic field B exists into the page. The bar starts to move from the vertex at time $t=0$ with a constant velocity. If the induced EMF is $\mathrm{E} \propto \mathrm{t}^{\mathrm{n}}$, then value of n is _.
solution:
As the bar moves without change in orientation, the length of bar will be proportional to its distance from the vertex.
i.e. $I=c(v t)$
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { induced emf } E=B / v \\
& =c B v^2 t
\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow n=1$

q: A coil of inductance $300 \mathrm{mH}$ and resistance $2 \Omega$ is connected to a source of voltage 2V. The current reaches half of its steady state value in
question: A coil of inductance $300 \mathrm{mH}$ and resistance $2 \Omega$ is connected to a source of voltage 2V. The current reaches half of its steady state value in
solution:
$\mathrm{I}=\mathrm{I}_{\circ}\left(1-\mathrm{e}^{-\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{L}}\mathrm{t}}\right)$
$0.693=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{L}} \mathrm{t}$
$\mathrm{t}=\frac{.3 \times 0.693}{2}=0.1 \mathrm{sec}$

q: A coil having $n$ turns and resistance $4 \mathrm{R} \Omega$. This combination is moved in time $t$ seconds from a magnetic field $W_1$ weber to $W_2$ weber. The induced current in the circuit is
question: A coil having $n$ turns and resistance $4 \mathrm{R} \Omega$. This combination is moved in time $t$ seconds from a magnetic field $W_1$ weber to $W_2$ weber. The induced current in the circuit is
solution:
$\mathrm{I}=-\frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{R}^{\prime}} \frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}$
or, I $=-\frac{1}{R^{\prime}} \mathrm{n}\left[\frac{\mathrm{W}_2-\mathrm{W}_1}{\mathrm{t}_2-\mathrm{t}_1}\right]$
( $W_1$ and $W_2$ are not the magnetic field, but the values of flux associated with one turn of coil) $I=\frac{-1}{(R+4 R)} \frac{n\left(W_2-W_1\right)}{t}$
or, $\mathrm{I}=-\frac{\mathrm{n}\left(\mathrm{W}_2-\mathrm{W}_1\right)}{5 \mathrm{Rt}}$

question: A square loop of side $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ being moved towards right at a constant speed of 2 $\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{s}$ as shown in figure. The front edge enters the $50 \mathrm{~cm}$ wide magnetic field at $t=$ 0 . The value of induced emf in the loop at $t=10 \mathrm{~s}$ will be :
solution: At $\mathrm{t}=10 \mathrm{sec}$ complete loop is in magnetic field therefore no change in flux $\mathrm{e}=\frac{\mathrm{d} \phi}{\mathrm{dt}}=0$ $\mathrm{e}=0$ for complete loop

subject: Physics
q:
A 20 cm long metallic rod is rotated with 210 rpm about an axis normal to the rod passing through its one end. The other end of the rod is in contact with a circular metallic ring. A constant and uniform magnetic field 0.2 T parallel to the axis exists everywhere. The emf developed between the centre and the ring is _________mV.

(Take π=227)
question:
A 20 cm long metallic rod is rotated with 210 rpm about an axis normal to the rod passing through its one end. The other end of the rod is in contact with a circular metallic ring. A constant and uniform magnetic field 0.2 T parallel to the axis exists everywhere. The emf developed between the centre and the ring is _________mV.

(Take π=227)
solution:
The data given is 

ω=210×2π60 rad s-1=7π  rad s-1l=0.2 mB=0.2 T

The emf is given by

ε=Bωl22

=0.2×7π×0.222

=88 mV

subject: Physics
q: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math> and power factor of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When capacitor with capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub></math> is put in series, the power factor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is
question: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math> and power factor of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When capacitor with capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub></math> is put in series, the power factor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is
solution:
In case of L-R circuit

Z=XL2+R2 Therefore, power factor

P1=cosϕ=RZ

As XL=R ⇒Z=2R

⇒P1=R2R⇒P1=12

In case of  L-C-R circuit

Z=R2+XL-XC2

According to the question XL=XC ⇒Z=R  ⇒P2=cosϕ=RR=1

⇒P1P2=12

subject: Physics
q: A coil is placed in a time varying magnetic field. If the number of turns in the coil were to be halved and the radius of wire doubled, the electrical power dissipated due to the current induced in the coil would be (Assume the coil to be short circuited.)
question: A coil is placed in a time varying magnetic field. If the number of turns in the coil were to be halved and the radius of wire doubled, the electrical power dissipated due to the current induced in the coil would be (Assume the coil to be short circuited.)
solution:
\(\mathrm{P}=\frac{\varepsilon^2}{R}\)
where, \(\varepsilon=\) inducedemf \(=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}\)
\(\phi=\mathrm{NBA}\)
\(\varepsilon=\mathrm{NA} \frac{\mathrm{dB}}{\mathrm{dt}}\)
also, \(R \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{r}^2}\)
where, \(\mathrm{R}=\) resistance and \(\mathrm{r}=\) radius of wire
\(\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P} \propto \frac{\mathrm{N}^2 \mathrm{r}^2}{\mathrm{H}^2} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{P}_1}{\mathrm{P}_2}=1 \\ & \mathrm{P}_1=\mathrm{P}_2 \end{aligned}\)
This means if the number of turns were quadrupled and the wire radius halved, the electrical power dissipated would be remains same.

question: In an $\mathrm{AC}$ generator, a coil with $\mathrm{N}$ turns, all of the same area $\mathrm{A}$ and total resistance $\mathrm{R}$, rotates with frequency $\omega$ in a magnetic field $B$. The maximum value of emf generated in the coil is "
solution: NBA $\omega$

subject: Physics
q: A simple pendulum made of mass 10 g and a metallic wire of length 10 cm is suspended vertically in a uniform magnetic field of 2 T. The magnetic field direction is perpendicular to the plane of oscillations of the pendulum. If the pendulum is released from an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with vertical, then maximum induced EMF between the point of suspension and point of oscillation is $\_\_\_\_$ mV. (Take $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
question: A simple pendulum made of mass 10 g and a metallic wire of length 10 cm is suspended vertically in a uniform magnetic field of 2 T. The magnetic field direction is perpendicular to the plane of oscillations of the pendulum. If the pendulum is released from an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with vertical, then maximum induced EMF between the point of suspension and point of oscillation is $\_\_\_\_$ mV. (Take $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^{2}$)
solution:
The maximum induced EMF for a rod of length $\ell$ rotating in a magnetic field $B$ is:
$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{B \omega_{max} \ell^2}{2} \quad \cdots(1)$

Using energy conservation to find $\omega_{max}$:
$mg\ell(1 - \cos 60°) = \frac{1}{2}(m\ell^2)\omega_m^2$

$\omega_m = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\ell}} = \sqrt{\frac{10}{0.1}} = 10 \text{ rad/s}$

From equation (1):
$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{2 \times 10 \times 0.01}{2} = 0.1 \text{ V} = 100 \text{ mV}$

subject: Physics
q: A 1 m long metal rod XY completes the circuit as shown in figure. The plane of the circuit is perpendicular to the magnetic field of flux density 0.15 T. If the resistance of the circuit is 5 Ω, the force needed to move the rod in direction, as indicated, with a constant speed of 4 m s-1 will be _______10–3 N.
question: A 1 m long metal rod XY completes the circuit as shown in figure. The plane of the circuit is perpendicular to the magnetic field of flux density 0.15 T. If the resistance of the circuit is 5 Ω, the force needed to move the rod in direction, as indicated, with a constant speed of 4 m s-1 will be _______10–3 N.
solution:
The force on the rod is given by

F=ilB

⇒F=BvlR×lB

⇒F=B2l2vR   ...(i)

The data given is

R=5 Ωl=1 mv=4 m s-1B=0.15 T

Substituting the values in equation (i)

F=0.152×12×45

=18×10-3 N

subject: Physics
q: A conducting circular loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.4 T with its plane perpendicular to the field. Somehow, the radius of the loop starts expanding at a constant rate of 1 mm s-1. The magnitude of induced emf in the loop at an instant when the radius of the loop is 2 cm will be _______ μV.
question: A conducting circular loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.4 T with its plane perpendicular to the field. Somehow, the radius of the loop starts expanding at a constant rate of 1 mm s-1. The magnitude of induced emf in the loop at an instant when the radius of the loop is 2 cm will be _______ μV.
solution:
The data given is 

B=0.4 TR=2×10-2 m

It is given that dRdt=10-3 m s-1

Area of the loop is A=πR2

So, dAdt=2πRdRdt

The flux is,

ϕ=2πRdRdt×B

So, the magnitude of emf is 

ε=2×227×2×10-2×10-3×0.4

=5.028×10-5 V=50.28 μV≈50  μV

The potential difference between the two faces of the cube, perpendicular to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math>-axis, is
question: A solid metal cube of edge length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> is moving in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math>-direction, at a constant speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. There is a uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>0.1</mtext><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math>-direction. The potential difference between the two faces of the cube, perpendicular to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math>-axis, is
solution:
emf developed is given by, e=Bvl

∴e=0.12×10-2×6⇒e=12×10-3=12 mV

subject: Physics
q: When the current changes from $+2 \mathrm{~A}$ to $-2 \mathrm{~A}$ in $0.05$ second, an e.m.f. of $8 \mathrm{~V}$ is induced in a coil. The coefficient of self-induction of the coil is
question: When the current changes from $+2 \mathrm{~A}$ to $-2 \mathrm{~A}$ in $0.05$ second, an e.m.f. of $8 \mathrm{~V}$ is induced in a coil. The coefficient of self-induction of the coil is
solution: $e=-\mathrm{L} \frac{\mathrm{dl}}{\mathrm{dt}} \Rightarrow 8=(\mathrm{L}) \frac{2-(-2)}{0.05} \Rightarrow \mathrm{L}=0.1 \mathrm{H}$

subject: Physics
q: $X P Q Y$ is a vertical smooth long loop having a total resistance $R$ where $P X$ is parallel to $Q Y$ and separation between them is $l$. A constant magnetic field $B$ perpendicular to the plane of the loop exists in the entire space. A rod $C D$ of length $L(L>l)$ and mass $m$ is made to slide down from rest under the gravity as shown in figure. The terminal speed acquired by the rod is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}.(\mathrm{g}=$ acceleration due to gravity)
question: $X P Q Y$ is a vertical smooth long loop having a total resistance $R$ where $P X$ is parallel to $Q Y$ and separation between them is $l$. A constant magnetic field $B$ perpendicular to the plane of the loop exists in the entire space. A rod $C D$ of length $L(L>l)$ and mass $m$ is made to slide down from rest under the gravity as shown in figure. The terminal speed acquired by the rod is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}.(\mathrm{g}=$ acceleration due to gravity)
solution:
As rod CD slides down with velocity $v$, it cuts magnetic field lines.
Induced EMF: $\varepsilon = BLv$
Induced current in the circuit: $I = \frac{BLv}{R}$
Magnetic force on the rod (upward, by Lenz's law):
$F_{mag} = BIL = \frac{B^2L^2v}{R}$
At terminal velocity, acceleration is zero.
Applying Newton's second law:
$mg - F_{mag} = 0$
$mg = \frac{B^2L^2v_{terminal}}{R}$
$v_{terminal} = \frac{mgR}{B^2L^2}$

subject: Physics
q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long horizontal wire extends from North East to South West. It is falling with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> at right angles to the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The value of the induced emf in the wire is:
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> long horizontal wire extends from North East to South West. It is falling with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5.0</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> at right angles to the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Wb</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The value of the induced emf in the wire is:
solution:
The wire makes an angle 45° with the magnetic field lines. Therefore, the induced emf will be,

ε=Bvlsin450

=0.3×10-4×5×10×12

=1.1×10-3 V

subject: Physics
q: A coil of cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> turns is placed in a uniform magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. When it is rotated with an angular velocity <math><mi>ω,</mi></math> the maximum e.m.f. induced in the coil will be:
question: A coil of cross-sectional area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> turns is placed in a uniform magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>. When it is rotated with an angular velocity <math><mi>ω,</mi></math> the maximum e.m.f. induced in the coil will be:
solution:
Magnetic flux through a coil of area A of n turns at an angle θ to the magnetic field of magnitude B is, φ=nBA cosθ=nBAcosωt. The induced emf,

ε=dϕdt=nBAωsin⁡ωt. Since, the maximum value of the sine function is 1,

εmax=nBAω

subject: Physics
q: A conducting circular loop of area $1.0 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field which varies as $B=\sin (100 t)$ Tesla. If the resistance of the loop is $100 \Omega$, then the average thermal energy dissipated in the loop in one period is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
question: A conducting circular loop of area $1.0 \mathrm{~m}^{2}$ is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field which varies as $B=\sin (100 t)$ Tesla. If the resistance of the loop is $100 \Omega$, then the average thermal energy dissipated in the loop in one period is $\_\_\_\_$ J.
solution:
Flux: $\phi = BA = \sin(100t)$ Wb
Induced EMF: $\varepsilon = -\frac{d\phi}{dt} = -100\cos(100t)$ V
Induced current: $I = \frac{\varepsilon}{R} = -\cos(100t)$ A
Power dissipated: $P = I^2R = 100\cos^2(100t)$ W
Average power: $P_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 = 50$ W
Time period: $T = \frac{2\pi}{100}$ s
Energy in one period: $E = P_{avg} \times T = 50 \times \frac{2\pi}{100} = \pi$ J

subject: Physics
q: Two coils of self inductance L1 and L2 are connected in series combination having mutual inductance of the coils as M. The equivalent self inductance of the combination will be
question: Two coils of self inductance L1 and L2 are connected in series combination having mutual inductance of the coils as M. The equivalent self inductance of the combination will be
solution:
Here self inductances are in series but their mutual inductances are linked oppositely.

As the inductors connected are in series opposition, hence magnetic flux will be opposing each other. Therefore,

Leq=L1+L2-2M

subject: Physics
q:
A metallic rod of length L is rotated with an angular speed of ω normal to a uniform magnetic field B about an axis passing through one end of rod as shown in figure. The induced emf will be :

.
question:
A metallic rod of length L is rotated with an angular speed of ω normal to a uniform magnetic field B about an axis passing through one end of rod as shown in figure. The induced emf will be :

.
solution:
Linear velocity at the distance x from the centre can be written as, v=ωx.

Therefore, induced EMF due to small element will be,

dε=Bvdx=Bωxdx

Integrating both sides, we get

∫dε=∫Bωxdx

⇒ε=Bω∫0Lxdx=BωL22
